verses.text: Now the sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel, (for he was the firstborn; but, forasmuch as he defiled his father's bed, his birthright was given unto the sons of Joseph the son of Israel: and the genealogy is not to be reckoned after the birthright. For Judah prevailed above his brethren, and of him came the chief ruler; but the birthright was Joseph's:) Guidance: The Chronicler pauses, before listing Reuben's descendants, to explain a great reversal: Reuben was the firstborn of Israel by birth, but 'forasmuch as he defiled his father's bed' (his sin with Bilhah, Gen 35:22), his birthright was taken from him and given to the sons of Joseph — while the role of ruler and the royal line passed to Judah. So the rights of the firstborn were split: the double portion went to Joseph, but the leadership and the scepter went to Judah, 'and of him came the chief ruler.' The chapter is showing us the moral seriousness of the firstborn's sin and the sovereign way the LORD redistributes blessing. Reuben's privilege was forfeited not by accident but by his own grievous sin; position and standing among God's people are not held by birthright alone but are bound up with character and faithfulness. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is not bound by human convention — the firstborn does not automatically inherit; the LORD bestows His chief blessings according to His own wisdom and purpose, and He had purposed that the ruler should come from Judah (the line of David and of Messiah, fulfilling Jacob's prophecy, Gen 49:8-10). The chapter is teaching us, soberly, that a high calling can be lost through sin: Reuben squandered his place. And it is teaching us, gloriously, that the LORD's redemptive purpose advanced through Judah, the tribe of the King — that out of all the brothers, the LORD chose the line through which the chief Ruler, Christ, would come. Privilege forfeited by one, sovereignly bestowed on another: this is the LORD's freedom in grace and judgment, written into the very structure of the genealogy.

For Judah prevailed above his brethren, and of him came the chief ruler; but the birthright was Joseph's:) Guidance: The Chronicler pauses, before listing Reuben's descendants, to explain a great reversal: Reuben was the firstborn of Israel by birth, but 'forasmuch as he defiled his father's bed' (his sin with Bilhah, Gen 35:22), his birthright was taken from him and given to the sons of Joseph — while the role of ruler and the royal line passed to Judah. The chapter is showing us the moral seriousness of the firstborn's sin and the sovereign way the LORD redistributes blessing. Reuben's privilege was forfeited not by accident but by his own grievous sin; position and standing among God's people are not held by birthright alone but are bound up with character and faithfulness. The chapter is teaching us, soberly, that a high calling can be lost through sin: Reuben squandered his place. The sons, I say, of Reuben the firstborn of Israel were, Hanoch, and Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi. The sons of Joel; Shemaiah his son, Gog his son, Shimei his son, Micah his son, Reaia his son, Baal his son, Beerah his son, whom Tilgathpilneser king of Assyria carried away captive: he was prince of the Reubenites. And his brethren by their families, when the genealogy of their generations was reckoned, were the chief, Jeiel, and Zechariah, And Bela the son of Azaz, the son of Shema, the son of Joel, who dwelt in Aroer, even unto Nebo and Baalmeon: And eastward he inhabited unto the entering in of the wilderness from the river Euphrates: because their cattle were multiplied in the land of Gilead. And in the days of Saul they made war with the Hagarites, who fell by their hand: and they dwelt in their tents throughout all the east land of Gilead. Guidance: The Chronicler records Reuben's descendants and their territory east of the Jordan — the broad pasture-lands of Gilead, where 'their cattle were multiplied,' stretching toward the wilderness and the Euphrates. But a shadow falls across the list: Beerah, the prince of the Reubenites, 'whom Tilgathpilneser king of Assyria carried away captive.' Even as the Chronicler names the families and their wide holdings, he marks the end toward which they were heading — the Assyrian exile. The chapter is showing us a people prosperous in flocks and lands, expanding eastward, victorious in the days of Saul over the Hagarites, yet finally carried away captive. The chapter is teaching us the fragility of earthly prosperity apart from faithfulness: Reuben's tribe had room, cattle, and victories, yet they were among the first to be torn from the land by Assyria. The chapter is teaching us that material increase is no guarantee of security; the broad pastures of Gilead could not hold a people who, as the chapter will soon say, turned from the God of their fathers. The chapter is teaching us to hold the good things of this world loosely, and to seek first the LORD whose favor alone secures our place — for lands and herds remain, but the unfaithful are carried away.

And the children of Gad dwelt over against them, in the land of Bashan unto Salcah: Joel the chief, and Shapham the next, and Jaanai, and Shaphat in Bashan. And their brethren of the house of their fathers were, Michael, and Meshullam, and Sheba, and Jorai, and Jachan, and Zia, and Heber, seven. These are the children of Abihail the son of Huri, the son of Jaroah, the son of Gilead, the son of Michael, the son of Jeshishai, the son of Jahdo, the son of Buz; Ahi the son of Abdiel, the son of Guni, chief of the house of their fathers. And they dwelt in Gilead in Bashan, and in her towns, and in all the suburbs of Sharon, upon their borders. All these were reckoned by genealogies in the days of Jotham king of Judah, and in the days of Jeroboam king of Israel. Guidance: The Chronicler turns to Gad, the tribe that settled beside Reuben in Bashan and Gilead, and records their chiefs and families, carefully noting that 'all these were reckoned by genealogies in the days of Jotham king of Judah, and in the days of Jeroboam king of Israel.' The chapter is showing us the Chronicler's painstaking care to preserve the records of even the eastern tribes, the ones farthest from Jerusalem and the temple, the ones soonest lost to exile. The chapter is teaching us that these Transjordan tribes, though they lay across the river and apart from the heart of the nation, were still fully Israel — counted, named, and remembered among the people of God. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community that the LORD kept the account even of the scattered and the lost; the men of Gad in distant Bashan had their place in the genealogy of Israel. The careful dating 'in the days of Jotham... and Jeroboam' anchors these records in real history and reminds us that the LORD's people lived under the divided monarchy, the eastern tribes nominally part of the northern kingdom yet recorded here alongside Judah's reckoning. The chapter is teaching us that no member of God's covenant family is too remote to be numbered; the LORD's care reaches across every river and border to His own.

And the children of Gad dwelt over against them, in the land of Bashan unto Salcah: Joel the chief, and Shapham the next, and Jaanai, and Shaphat in Bashan. And their brethren of the house of their fathers were, Michael, and Meshullam, and Sheba, and Jorai, and Jachan, and Zia, and Heber, seven. These are the children of Abihail the son of Huri, the son of Jaroah, the son of Gilead, the son of Michael, the son of Jeshishai, the son of Jahdo, the son of Buz; Ahi the son of Abdiel, the son of Guni, chief of the house of their fathers. And they dwelt in Gilead in Bashan, and in her towns, and in all the suburbs of Sharon, upon their borders. All these were reckoned by genealogies in the days of Jotham king of Judah, and in the days of Jeroboam king of Israel. Guidance: The Chronicler turns to Gad, the tribe that settled beside Reuben in Bashan and Gilead, and records their chiefs and families, carefully noting that 'all these were reckoned by genealogies in the days of Jotham king of Judah, and in the days of Jeroboam king of Israel.' The chapter is showing us the Chronicler's painstaking care to preserve the records of even the eastern tribes, the ones farthest from Jerusalem and the temple, the ones soonest lost to exile. The chapter is teaching us that these Transjordan tribes, though they lay across the river and apart from the heart of the nation, were still fully Israel — counted, named, and remembered among the people of God. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community that the LORD kept the account even of the scattered and the lost; the men of Gad in distant Bashan had their place in the genealogy of Israel. The careful dating 'in the days of Jotham... and Jeroboam' anchors these records in real history and reminds us that the LORD's people lived under the divided monarchy, the eastern tribes nominally part of the northern kingdom yet recorded here alongside Judah's reckoning. The chapter is teaching us that no member of God's covenant family is too remote to be numbered; the LORD's care reaches across every river and border to His own.

The sons of Reuben, and the Gadites, and half the tribe of Manasseh, of valiant men, men able to bear buckler and sword, and to shoot with bow, and skilful in war, were four and forty thousand seven hundred and threescore, that went out to the war. And they made war with the Hagarites, with Jetur, and Nephish, and Nodab. And they were helped against them, and the Hagarites were delivered into their hand, and all that were with them: for they cried to God in the battle, and he was intreated of them; because they put their trust in him. And they took away their cattle; of their camels fifty thousand, and of sheep two hundred and fifty thousand, and of asses two thousand, and of men an hundred thousand. For there fell down many slain, because the war was of God. And they dwelt in their steads until the captivity. Guidance: Here is the spiritual high point of the chapter. The eastern tribes — Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh — fielded 44,760 valiant, skilled warriors and went to war against the Hagarites. And the Chronicler tells us plainly why they won: 'And they were helped against them... for they cried to God in the battle, and he was intreated of them; because they put their trust in him.' They prayed in the thick of the fight, they trusted the LORD, and He answered: 'there fell down many slain, because the war was of God.' The chapter is showing us the true secret of victory — not the number of warriors nor their skill with bow and sword, but crying out to God and trusting in Him. These men were 'mighty men of valour,' yet they did not lean on their own strength; in the battle they called on the LORD, and He fought for them. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD helps those who cry to Him and put their trust in Him; the prayer of faith, even in the heat of conflict, is heard and answered. The chapter is teaching us that our victories are 'of God' — when we triumph in any good fight, it is because the LORD has helped us, not because of our own might. The chapter is teaching us to do as these men did: to be diligent and skilful in our duty ('men able to bear buckler and sword'), yet to lean wholly on God, crying to Him and trusting Him in the very moment of trial. The God who was intreated of Reuben and Gad in their battle is the same God who invites every believer to cast their cares upon Him and find help in time of need. Trust Him in the battle, and the victory will be His to give and His to be praised for.

And the children of the half tribe of Manasseh dwelt in the land: they increased from Bashan unto Baalhermon and Senir, and unto mount Hermon. And these were the heads of the house of their fathers, even Epher, and Ishi, and Eliel, and Azriel, and Jeremiah, and Hodaviah, and Jahdiel, mighty men of valour, famous men, and heads of the house of their fathers. And they transgressed against the God of their fathers, and went a whoring after the gods of the people of the land, whom God destroyed before them. And the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Pul king of Assyria, and the spirit of Tilgathpilneser king of Assyria, and he carried them away, even the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh, and brought them unto Halah, and Habor, and Hara, and to the river Gozan, unto this day. Guidance: The chapter ends in sorrow and judgment. The half-tribe of Manasseh were numerous, 'mighty men of valour, famous men' — and yet: 'they transgressed against the God of their fathers, and went a whoring after the gods of the people of the land, whom God destroyed before them.' Here is the bitter irony — they took up the very idols of the nations the LORD had destroyed to give them the land. And so the judgment fell: 'the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Pul king of Assyria... and he carried them away.' The chapter is showing us, in the very same breath, the height of their honour and the depth of their fall: famous, valiant men who turned to idols and were carried into exile. The chapter is teaching us the deadly progression of apostasy: they forsook 'the God of their fathers' and chased after the gods of the people around them — the slow seduction of conforming to the culture, of worshiping what the neighbors worshiped — and it ended in ruin. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD sovereignly governs even the empires that judge His people: it was God who 'stirred up the spirit' of the Assyrian kings; the great powers were instruments in His hand to discipline His unfaithful people. The chapter is teaching us, as a solemn warning, that no measure of strength, fame, or past blessing can shield a people who abandon the LORD for idols; the same God who helped them in battle when they trusted Him (v20) gave them over to the enemy when they betrayed Him. The eastern tribes had cried to God and been delivered; now they whored after other gods and were carried away. Let every reader take heed: faithfulness, not might or memory, is the condition of abiding in the LORD's blessing — 'be not highminded, but fear' (Rom 11:20).
